desc: In 2015, some 885,000 migrants arrived in the EU via the Eastern Mediterranean route – 17 times the number in in 2014, which was itself a record year. The vast majority of them arrived on several Greek islands, most on Lesbos. The numbers increased gradually from January to March, but began to climb in April, peaking at 216 000 in October. The numbers eased slightly in November and December with the onset of winter, but were still well above the figures from the same months of 2014.

Throughout 2015 Frontex deployed an increased number of officers and vessels to the Greek islands to assist in patrolling the sea and registering the thousands of migrants arriving daily. In December, the agency launched Poseidon Rapid Intervention after the Greek authorities requested additional assistance at its borders.

Most of the migrants on this route in 2015 originated from Syria, followed by Afghanistan and Somalia. There are also increasing numbers of migrants coming from sub-Saharan Africa. Most of the migrants continued their journeys north, leaving Greece through its border with the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia. Frontex also deploys officers at Greece's northern land border to assist in registering exiting migrants.

Note: IOM tracks deaths along the full Eastern Mediterranean corridor, which extends from the Turkey-Greece sea crossing south through Egypt, the Sinai Peninsula, and Sudan. Incidents appearing in Egypt and Sudan reflect migrants transiting northward toward Turkey or the Mediterranean coast. The 'Türkiye-Europe land route' and 'Iran to Türkiye' sub-routes are also included, covering the overland journey through eastern Turkey toward Europe.

The Central Mediterranean route remained under intense migratory pressure in 2015, although the total number of migrants arriving in Italy fell to 154 000 - about a tenth lower than the record set in 2014. The main reasons for the drop were the shift of Syrians to the Eastern Mediterranean route and a shortage of boats faced by smugglers in the latter part of the year. Smuggling networks remain well established in Libya, where migrants gather before crossing the sea. In 2015 Eritreans, Nigerians and Somalis accounted for the biggest share of the migrants making the dangerous journey.

People smugglers typically put migrants aboard old, unseaworthy fishing boats, or even small rubber dinghies, which are much overloaded and thus prone to capsizing. These vessels are generally equipped with poor engines, lack proper navigation systems and often have insufficient fuel to reach Europe. For these reasons, the vast majority of border control operations in the Central Mediterranean turn into Search and Rescue (SAR) operations.

Note: IOM classifies Sahara Desert transit deaths as part of the Central Mediterranean route, as these migrants are typically heading toward Libya and onward to Italy. This means incidents appear across a wide geographic area — from Niger, Mali, and Chad in the south, through Algeria and Libya, to the Mediterranean Sea. The Sahara crossing is one of the deadliest segments of this route, with deaths from dehydration, exposure, and vehicle abandonment. These same Saharan regions overlap geographically with the Western African route, which tracks migration toward the Atlantic coast and Canary Islands.

The Morocco to Spain route had been a noted pressure point for years – certainly since 2005, when thousands of sub-Saharan migrants made world headlines by trying to climb over the fence in the Spanish enclave of Melilla.

Co-operation between Spain and Morocco has since kept migrant numbers comparatively low on this route. Migrants are also more inclined to depart from Libya because the likelihood of being returned by EU authorities is much lower.

A decade ago, migrants from Morocco to Spain were typically economic ones from Algeria and Morocco, hoping for jobs in Spain, France and Italy. Since then, however, they have increasingly been joined by sub-Saharan Africans, driven northwards by conflicts in Mali, Sudan, South Sudan, Cameroon, Nigeria, Chad and the Central African Republic. In 2015, Syrians accounted for the biggest share of detections on this route.

West Africans reach Morocco or Algeria via two land routes. One follows the West African coastline; the shorter one crosses the Sahara. The coastal route is naturally preferred by migrants leaving Senegal and Mauritania, but also, often, by nationals of countries further afield - such as Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire or Benin - because the Sahara crossing is judged so dangerous.

There are various reasons for the fluctuation of overall numbers on this route. Spain has stepped up coastal patrols, installed the SIVE maritime surveillance system along its southern border and signed bilateral agreements with Mauritania and Senegal. It has also strengthened border checks at the main ports, a significant deterrent for would-be migrants secreting themselves aboard trucks and containers on ferries headed to Almeria and Algeciras – the traditional method of irregular entry. Rising unemployment in Spain, and therefore fewer opportunities for migrant workers, is also thought to be a factor.

Spanish border authorities do not only face challenges from irregular migrants. The Western Mediterranean route has also long been a major conduit for drug-smugglers moving cannabis and cocaine towards the lucrative markets of the EU. Evidence suggests that the speedboats traditionally deployed by the smugglers are increasingly being replaced by small planes and helicopters.

Note: Incidents on this route span from the Moroccan and Algerian coasts across the sea to Spain, and inland through northern Morocco and the Spanish enclaves of Ceuta and Melilla. The 'Italy to France' sub-route is also included, covering deaths along the Alpine border crossing between Italy and France.

Disclaimer: Data presented refer to the number of detections of illegal border-crossing at the external borders of the European Union. Illegal border-crossings at the external borders may be attempted several times by the same person.

The record number of migrants arriving in Greece had a direct knock-on effect on the Western Balkan route, as the people who entered the EU in Greece tried to make their way via the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia, Serbia into Hungary and Croatia and then towards western Europe. This led to unprecedented numbers of migrants seeking to re-enter the EU through Hungary's borders with Serbia. After Hungary completed the construction of a fence on its border with Serbia in September, the flow of migrants shifted to Croatia. In all of 2015, the region recorded 764 000 detections of illegal border crossings by migrants, a 16-fold rise from 2014. The top-ranking nationality was Syrian, followed by Iraqis and Afghans. Earlier in the year, unprecedented numbers of Kosovo* nationals crossed the Serbian-Hungarian border illegally.

Note: IOM tracks deaths along the full Western Balkans corridor, which extends from Greece northward through North Macedonia, Serbia, Hungary, Croatia, and into destination countries including Austria, Germany, and northeastern Italy (via Slovenia). Incidents appearing in Italy, Germany, or Czechia reflect migrants who died after arriving in these countries via the Balkans route. The Turkey-Bulgaria-Greece border triangle is a major crossing zone and appears prominently on this route.
*This designation is without prejudice to positions on status, and is in line with UNSCR 1244 and the ICJ Opinion on the Kosovo declaration of independence.

The European Union's 6 000-kilometre-long land border between Belarus, Moldova, Ukraine, the Russian Federation and its eastern Member States (Estonia, Finland, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Norway, Poland, Slovakia, Bulgaria and Romania) presents significant challenges for border control.

Overall the scale of irregular migration at all the eastern borders is much smaller than on any other migratory route into the EU and amounts to a fraction of a percent (0.1%) of the total. Until 2015, Ukraine was the main transit country both for the citizens of the CIS (mainly Georgians and Russians), and non-CIS irregular migrants (primarily Somali and Afghan nationals) aiming to reach the EU through its eastern borders. In addition, Ukraine is also a major route for migrants from the Caucasus region and Central Asian countries travelling towards (or from) the Russian Federation.

In 2015 a new migrant route appeared to open up: the so-called Arctic route through Russia over the land borders with Norway and Finland. About 6000 asylum applicants were counted between October and December. Most were originally from Afghanistan and Syria, although recently they have been joined by migrants of other nationalities. The main crossing point was Storskog, the only legal land border crossing between Norway and Russia, which saw 5 200 applications for asylum in 2015, compared to less than 10 the previous year. Migrants took to using bicycles to negotiate the border zone because pedestrian traffic is banned and drivers are fined if they give lifts to passengers without proper documents.

The situation eased in Norway in December. Migrants were deterred by worsening weather conditions and even by the shortage of bicycles. The Russian authorities have also begun refusing travellers the permission to transit their country without Schengen visas. However, there has been a clear dis­placement to the Finnish border. The total number of arrivals to the Finnish two most northern border crossing points of Salla and Raja-Jooseppi, by mid-January 2016 was roughly 1 000 persons.

After border crossing by bicycles was banned, migrants now acquire cars and drive to the border by themselves. Some of the migrants have lived in the Rus­sian Federation for a long period of time. Lately the share of those who were only in transit through the Russian Federa­tion has been growing, indicating that the knowledge about the route in the or­igin countries is spreading.

Worth noting is the introduction of a series of local border-traffic agreements between Ukraine and its European Union neighbours (Hungary, Poland, Slovakia), which allowed holders of local border traffic permits to stay for up to 90 days per half-year. While it is difficult to perform control along the 30–50-kilometre non-demarcated zone where the agreements are in force, Member States reported only a few isolated breaches. In fact, most of the issues seem to concern small scale smuggling of excise goods. Along the Eastern land border, abuse of legal travel channels is more common than illegal border-crossing, mainly by citizens of the CIS (Commonwealth of Independent States) including Georgians.

Note: Incidents on this route span the EU's eastern frontier from Finland and the Arctic in the north to the Poland-Belarus and Lithuania-Belarus borders. The Arctic route through Scandinavia (including northern Sweden) is included, as migrants used this corridor to enter the EU via Norway and Finland.
Since Frontex data collection begun in 2008, numbers of illegal border crossings remained stable, oscillating around 1,500 each year.

The route between Senegal, Mauritania and Morocco and the Spanish Canary Islands was once the busiest irregular entry point for the whole of Europe, peaking at 32 000 migrants arriving on the islands in 2006.

But the numbers dropped by 60 per cent in 2007 following bilateral agreements between Spain and Senegal and Mauritania, including repatriation agreements. Strengthened border controls, including the installation of the SIVE maritime surveillance system, also helped, along with the Frontex-coordinated Operation Hera.

Migrants on this route were mostly from Morocco and Senegal, with others from Niger, Nigeria and Mali. They generally travelled in long wooden fishing boats, known as cayucos; migrants from Morocco use smaller fishing boats called pateras.
The numbers continued to drop from 2007, until by 2012 there were just 170 arrivals in the Canaries. The figure remained stable for the next two years, although it rose to 874 in 2015.

This route also encompasses the overland Sahara transit corridor through West and Central Africa. Migrants from countries such as Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, and Ivory Coast travel north through Burkina Faso, Niger, Mali, and Chad before reaching the Atlantic coast or North African departure points. Deaths along these inland transit routes — from dehydration, exposure, and vehicle accidents in the Sahara — are a significant and often underreported part of the Western African migration flow.

Note: Incidents span from the West African coast (Senegal, Mauritania, Western Sahara) across the Atlantic to the Canary Islands, and inland through the Sahel region. The Sahara transit zone overlaps geographically with the Central Mediterranean route — IOM classifies transit deaths under different routes depending on whether migrants were heading toward the Atlantic coast (Western African) or toward Libya (Central Mediterranean).

The Moroccan smuggler operation is not well developed. Sea passages tend to be arranged by individuals working independently, serving clients who have made their own way to the coast rather than using the services of organised networks. Small boats found on Lanzarote containing very small numbers of migrants gave strong indications that drug smuggling was the primary goal of these journeys.

The Americas migration route encompasses multiple dangerous pathways used by migrants and refugees seeking to reach the United States, Canada, or safety within Latin America. The US-Mexico border crossing is by far the deadliest segment, with thousands of deaths recorded in the Sonoran Desert, the Rio Grande, and along remote stretches of the border.

The Darien Gap — a roadless stretch of jungle between Colombia and Panama — has emerged as one of the most perilous migration corridors in the world, with record numbers of crossings since 2021. Migrants from Haiti, Venezuela, Cuba, and countries as far as West Africa and South Asia traverse this route despite extreme dangers including drowning, exposure, and violence.

Caribbean sea crossings — from Cuba, Haiti, and the Dominican Republic toward the US and Puerto Rico — continue to claim lives, with migrants using makeshift rafts and overcrowded boats across treacherous waters. Venezuelan migrants fleeing economic collapse have also opened new maritime routes through the southern Caribbean.

Note: This route covers the entire Western Hemisphere, from South America through Central America, the Caribbean, and across the US-Mexico border. Incidents appear across a vast geographic area reflecting the many pathways migrants use — the Darien Gap, Caribbean Sea crossings, Central American transit, the US-Mexico border zone, and even remote areas of South America where migrants begin their northward journey.
Data for this route is compiled from IOM's Missing Migrants Project, which tracks incidents of death and disappearance along migratory routes worldwide.

The Horn of Africa region — encompassing Somalia, Ethiopia, Eritrea, Djibouti, and surrounding areas — is one of the world's most active migration corridors. Hundreds of thousands of people move each year along the Eastern Route from the East and Horn of Africa (EHOA) toward the Gulf states, particularly Saudi Arabia via Yemen.

The journey from the Horn of Africa to Yemen across the Gulf of Aden and Red Sea remains extremely dangerous. Smugglers frequently overload boats and have been known to throw passengers overboard. Those who survive the sea crossing face further dangers in Yemen, including kidnapping, trafficking, and violence.

A northern route also exists, with migrants from Eritrea, Ethiopia, and Somalia traveling through Sudan toward Libya and the Mediterranean. This route intersects with the Sahara Desert crossing and feeds into the Central Mediterranean migration flow.

Note: Incidents on this route span from the Horn of Africa countries (Somalia, Ethiopia, Eritrea, Djibouti) across the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden to Yemen and the Arabian Peninsula. The route includes both the sea crossings and overland journeys through the region. Deaths in Yemen are prominently featured as it serves as a major transit and destination point for Horn of Africa migrants heading toward Saudi Arabia.

Migration within East and Southern Africa involves movement from the Great Lakes region and Central Africa toward Southern African countries, particularly South Africa. Migrants face dangerous border crossings, xenophobic violence, and exploitation by smugglers along these routes.

The route through Tanzania, Malawi, Mozambique, and Zimbabwe toward South Africa sees significant movement of refugees and economic migrants from the Democratic Republic of Congo, Burundi, and Rwanda.

Sea crossings to Mayotte — a French overseas territory in the Comoros archipelago — represent another deadly maritime route in the region, with migrants from Comoros and Madagascar attempting the crossing in small boats called kwassa-kwassa.

Note: IOM classifies incidents by the direction of migration, not the geographic location of the death. This means deaths occurring in Central Africa (DRC, Congo, Cameroon) appear on this route when the migrant was traveling south or east — toward Uganda, South Africa, or other East/Southern African destinations. The geographic spread of incidents reflects the full journey from Central African departure points to Southern African destinations.
Data for these routes is compiled from IOM's Missing Migrants Project.

The Iran-Afghanistan corridor is one of the deadliest and least documented migration routes in the world. An estimated 5,000 recorded deaths have occurred along this route, with the true number believed to be significantly higher.

Afghan migrants and refugees, fleeing decades of conflict, Taliban rule, and economic collapse, travel westward through Iran's eastern provinces. The journey through remote desert and mountainous terrain is extremely hazardous. Migrants face dehydration, exposure, drowning in border rivers, and violence from smugglers and border forces.

Iran hosts one of the largest refugee populations in the world, with millions of Afghans living in the country. Many use Iran as a transit point toward Turkey and onward to Europe via the Eastern Mediterranean route, creating a migration chain that connects Central Asia to the EU's borders.

Note: Incidents on this route cover the Afghanistan-Iran-Pakistan triangle, spanning from Afghanistan's western provinces through Iran to the Turkish border. The geographic spread reflects both the Afghanistan-to-Iran crossing and the broader Iranian transit zone. Some incidents in Pakistan's western border region are also included when migrants were heading toward Iran.
Data for this route is compiled from IOM's Missing Migrants Project, which has increasingly documented incidents in this historically underreported corridor.

The South & East Asia migration region encompasses dangerous routes across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and East Asia. Maritime migration centers on the Bay of Bengal and Andaman Sea, where Rohingya refugees from Myanmar and Bangladeshi migrants undertake perilous sea crossings toward Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand.

The Naf River, forming the border between Myanmar and Bangladesh, is a critical crossing point for Rohingya fleeing persecution. Overcrowded and unseaworthy boats frequently capsize, and migrants face exploitation by smugglers and human traffickers. The crisis intensified dramatically after 2017, when a military crackdown in Myanmar's Rakhine State drove over 700,000 Rohingya into Bangladesh.

This route also includes migration through the Afghanistan-Pakistan border region, Indonesian maritime crossings, and movements along the Korean Peninsula. Migrants from Afghanistan traversing Pakistan's tribal areas face extreme dangers, while sea crossings between Indonesian islands and toward Australia continue to claim lives.

Note: This route covers a vast geographic region from the Afghanistan-Pakistan border east through South and Southeast Asia to Indonesia and beyond. Incidents appear across the Bay of Bengal, Andaman Sea, and Indonesian archipelago, as well as inland routes through Myanmar, Bangladesh, and Pakistan. The wide geographic spread reflects the many distinct migration corridors grouped under this route.
Data for these routes is compiled from IOM's Missing Migrants Project, which tracks incidents across the broader South & East Asia region.

The English Channel crossing between France and the United Kingdom has become an increasingly significant migration route since 2018. Migrants, primarily from Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan, and East Africa, attempt the crossing in small boats and dinghies from beaches near Calais and Dunkirk.

The 33-kilometer Strait of Dover is one of the world's busiest shipping lanes, making small boat crossings extremely dangerous. Cold water temperatures, strong currents, and heavy maritime traffic create life-threatening conditions.

This route also includes deaths along the France-Italy border in the Alps, where migrants attempt mountain crossings in harsh winter conditions. The route grew significantly after the closure of the Calais 'Jungle' camp in 2016 and the tightening of security around the Channel Tunnel.

Note: Incidents on this route are concentrated in the English Channel strait and the northern France/Belgium coast, with some appearing along the France-Germany border and the Alpine crossing between France and Italy. The geographic area covers northwestern Europe where migrants gather before attempting the Channel crossing.
